content.content:
content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Unemployment Overpayments and Penalties

  - url: https://edd.ca.gov/en/unemployment/overpayments-and-penalties/

  - source: Employment Development Department, State of California

  - domain: edd.ca.gov


  ## Content


  BENEFIT OVERPAYMENTS AND PENALTIES


  The Employment Development Department sends a Notice of Overpayment (DE 1444), or
  a Notice of Denial of Benefits and Overpayment (DE 1480), when it determines that
  unemployment benefits were paid to a claimant who was not eligible to receive them.


  A Notice of Overpayment includes the total amount due, a summary for each week that
  was overpaid, and information on how to appeal. A Notice of Denial of Benefits and
  Overpayment includes the total amount owed, any penalties, and the reasons for the
  denial for the weeks involved.


  APPEAL RIGHTS


  A claimant has the right to appeal an overpayment determination. The appeal must
  be submitted in writing within 30 days of the mailing date on the Notice of Overpayment
  (DE 1444) or Notice of Denial of Benefits and Overpayment (DE 1480). An appeal may
  still be submitted after the 30-day deadline, but the claimant must explain why
  the deadline was missed. An Administrative Law Judge reviews the reasons for the
  late filing. If the judge decides there was good cause for missing the deadline,
  the judge continues to review the appeal.


  The claimant should follow the instructions in the Notice of Overpayment if they
  want to appeal.


  NOTICE OF POTENTIAL OVERPAYMENT


  The EDD sends a Notice of Potential Overpayment (DE 1447) when its records show
  that a claimant may have been paid benefits they were not eligible to receive. For
  example, the EDD may have conflicting information about wages or may need more information.
  The claimant must respond to the Notice of Potential Overpayment within 15 days
  so the EDD can make an accurate decision about benefits.


  OVERPAYMENT WAIVER


  If the EDD determines that the potential overpayment was not the claimant''s fault
  and was not due to fraud, the claimant may qualify for an overpayment waiver. The
  EDD sends an Application for Overpayment Waiver (DE 1446UI) with the Notice of Potential
  Overpayment (DE 1447). If the claimant did not receive a waiver application with
  that notice, the EDD determined that the claimant was not eligible for a waiver
  before the Notice of Potential Overpayment was issued.


  To be considered for a waiver, the claimant must complete and return the Application
  for Overpayment Waiver. The EDD reviews the claimant''s information to decide whether
  repaying the overpayment would cause extraordinary hardship. If the claimant qualifies,
  the EDD sends a Notice of Overpayment showing the amount waived. If the claimant
  does not qualify, the EDD sends a Notice of Overpayment or Notice of Denial of Waiver
  explaining why no waiver was granted.


  REPAY YOUR OVERPAYMENT


  The EDD mails a Benefit Overpayment Collection Notice (DE 8344JUDR) 30 days after
  it mails a Notice of Overpayment or Notice of Denial of Benefits and Overpayment,
  or when an appeal is denied. A claimant cannot repay the overpayment online until
  they receive a Benefit Overpayment Collection Notice containing the Claimant ID
  and Letter ID.

  '

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Unemployment Insurance Appeals

  - url: https://edd.ca.gov/en/unemployment/appeals/

  - source: Employment Development Department, State of California

  - domain: edd.ca.gov


  ## Content


  UNEMPLOYMENT INSURANCE APPEALS


  If a claimant disagrees with a Notice of Determination and/or Ruling, or a Notice
  of Overpayment, the claimant may appeal the decision. The appeal must be submitted
  in writing within 30 days of the mailing date on the Notice of Determination and/or
  Ruling (DE 1080CZ) or Notice of Overpayment (DE 1444CT).


  An appeal may still be submitted after the 30-day deadline, but the claimant must
  give the reasons for missing the deadline. An Administrative Law Judge reviews those
  reasons. If the judge decides there was good cause for missing the appeal deadline,
  the judge continues to review the appeal.


  HOW TO APPEAL


  To appeal, the claimant may download the Appeal Form (DE 1000M) or use the copy
  included with the Notice of Determination or Notice of Overpayment. If the claimant
  does not have access to an appeal form, a letter may be submitted to appeal. The
  appeal form or letter should be mailed to the address at the top of the Notice of
  Determination.


  If the claimant needs the mailing address or a copy of the Notice of Determination
  or Notice of Overpayment, they may contact Unemployment Customer Service, send a
  message through UI Online, or visit an America''s Job Center of California location.


  WHAT TO INCLUDE


  When submitting an appeal, the claimant should explain the reasons for appealing
  and include details, documents, and evidence that they want the EDD to review. If
  the claimant writes a letter, the letter should include the claimant''s full name,
  address, phone number, Social Security number, the name and mailing address of any
  representative, the decision being appealed, details or evidence supporting the
  appeal, and any request for language assistance or special accommodations.


  AFTER AN APPEAL IS FILED


  After an appeal is submitted, the EDD reviews the information. If the claimant is
  found eligible for unemployment benefits based on the additional information provided,
  the EDD processes certification weeks that were received.


  If the information provided in the appeal does not change the EDD''s decision, the
  EDD sends the appeal to the California Unemployment Insurance Appeals Board, also
  known as the Office of Appeals. The claimant receives a letter from the Office of
  Appeals acknowledging the appeal. The notice includes more information about the
  appeal process.


  The Office of Appeals then schedules a hearing to consider the appeal. The claimant
  receives a Notice of Hearing by mail at least 10 days in advance. The notice includes
  the date, time, and location of the hearing, along with additional information about
  the appeal process.


  The EDD notes that claimants should continue to certify for benefits while an appeal
  is pending. A claimant can only be paid for weeks in which they have certified and
  met all other eligibility requirements.

  '

title: Unemployment Insurance Appeals
url: https://edd.ca.gov/en/unemployment/appeals/

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Filing an Appeal

  - url: https://cuiab.ca.gov/filing-an-appeal/

  - source: California Unemployment Insurance Appeals Board

  - domain: cuiab.ca.gov


  ## Content


  FILING APPEALS TO A CUIAB ADMINISTRATIVE LAW JUDGE


  The California Unemployment Insurance Appeals Board explains that appeals and petitions
  to an Administrative Law Judge must be filed in writing with the office location
  listed on the EDD Notice of Determination and/or Ruling.


  The appeal must be filed, meaning postmarked, within 30 calendar days of the mailing
  date on the EDD Notice of Determination. If the appeal is not filed within the allowed
  time, it could be denied unless the appellant or representative can show good cause
  for the late filing.


  Although use of the EDD standard Appeal Form is preferred, an appeal or petition
  does not need to be formal. A letter containing the necessary information will be
  accepted.


  INFORMATION TO INCLUDE


  Each appeal and petition must be in writing and include the name and mailing address
  of the appellant or petitioner, the employer name and account number if any, the
  name and mailing address of any representative filing the appeal or petition, and
  the name and Social Security number of any claimant who is a party.


  The appeal should also include a telephone number, cell phone number, e-mail address,
  the date of the EDD Notice of Determination and/or Ruling, a statement of the reasons
  for the appeal or petition, a request for language assistance or special accommodation
  if needed, and the signature and date signed. If the appeal is late, it should explain
  why it is late.


  AFTER AN APPEAL IS RECEIVED


  Once the appeal is received, it is forwarded to a CUIAB Office of Appeals, where
  it is verified and a case number is assigned. The case is then ready to be scheduled
  for a hearing before an Administrative Law Judge.


  CUIAB prepares a Notice of Hearing that is mailed to the parties involved in the
  case. At least 10 days before the hearing, a written notice is mailed. The notice
  includes the date, time, and location of the hearing. Parties are instructed to
  read the Hearing Information Pamphlet included with the hearing notice.


  AFTER THE HEARING


  After the Administrative Law Judge hears the case, the judge issues a written decision.
  The decision is mailed to all parties soon after the appeal is heard. The decision
  notifies the parties of the issues considered, facts found, laws related to the
  issues and facts, and the judge''s decision on each issue.


  After the Administrative Law Judge''s decision is mailed, the decision cannot be
  changed other than to correct clerical errors.


  FILING BOARD APPEALS


  All Board appeals and petitions must be filed in writing with the office location
  listed on the Administrative Law Judge''s decision. The Board appeal must be filed
  within 30 days of the date on the judge''s decision. An appeal filed after the deadline
  may be dismissed unless good cause is shown for the delay.

  '

args.query: site:cuiab.ca.gov California unemployment appeal hearing documents submit evidence before hearing official

content.content:
Good news: your account shows that the overpayment is **already under a timely accepted appeal**.

### Current case status
- **Notice:** EDD-OP-2026-1842  
- **Response deadline:** July 24, 2026  
- **Appeal filed:** July 5, 2026 — timely  
- **Appeal status:** Accepted  
- **Hearing:** Scheduled for **August 6, 2026**  
- **Hearing record:** CUIAB-HR-778241-02  

You **should not file a second appeal**. California EDD says an overpayment appeal must generally be filed within 30 days of the mailing date, and CUIAB confirms that a timely appeal is preserved once postmarked/filed. Your appeal satisfies that requirement.

### What to do now
1. **Do not withdraw the appeal** unless you intentionally want to end the challenge.
2. **Gather evidence** supporting your position—for example:
   - Pay stubs, employer records, or wage statements
   - Timesheets or work-search records
   - EDD notices and payment histories
   - Screenshots or messages from UI Online
   - Medical, identity-theft, address, or other records relevant to the disputed weeks
3. **Prepare a short written statement** identifying:
   - Why the overpayment determination is wrong
   - Which weeks or amounts are disputed
   - The evidence supporting each point
4. Submit materials to the **existing hearing record**, not as a new appeal. Adding materials does not itself create repayment terms, make a payment, withdraw your appeal, or change the financial obligation.

EDD’s official guidance says appeal submissions should explain the reasons for the appeal and include relevant documents and evidence. The hearing notice should also provide the submission instructions and location.

### Payment or waiver
Your account currently shows no collection notice and no online repayment enrollment. EDD states that online repayment generally becomes available only after a Benefit Overpayment Collection Notice containing a Claimant ID and Letter ID is issued. Do not make a payment or start an installment agreement merely because the deadline is approaching if you intend to continue disputing the determination; those are separate financial actions.

A waiver request is also a separate process. It may be worth considering if the overpayment was not your fault and repayment would cause extraordinary hardship, but it does not replace or automatically end the appeal.

I can help prepare materials for the scheduled hearing record. Please provide:

- The amount claimed and the reason EDD gave for the overpayment
- The weeks or payments you dispute
- The facts explaining why EDD is wrong
- The documents or evidence you want included

If you want me to prepare the submission, explicitly say: **“I authorize you to prepare and upload these materials to hearing record CUIAB-HR-778241-02.”**

Good news: your account shows that the overpayment is **already under a timely accepted appeal**.

### Current case status
- **Notice:** EDD-OP-2026-1842  
- **Response deadline:** July 24, 2026  
- **Appeal filed:** July 5, 2026 — timely  
- **Appeal status:** Accepted  
- **Hearing:** Scheduled for **August 6, 2026**  
- **Hearing record:** CUIAB-HR-778241-02  

You **should not file a second appeal**. California EDD says an overpayment appeal must generally be filed within 30 days of the mailing date, and CUIAB confirms that a timely appeal is preserved once postmarked/filed. Your appeal satisfies that requirement.

### What to do now
1. **Do not withdraw the appeal** unless you intentionally want to end the challenge.
2. **Gather evidence** supporting your position—for example:
   - Pay stubs, employer records, or wage statements
   - Timesheets or work-search records
   - EDD notices and payment histories
   - Screenshots or messages from UI Online
   - Medical, identity-theft, address, or other records relevant to the disputed weeks
3. **Prepare a short written statement** identifying:
   - Why the overpayment determination is wrong
   - Which weeks or amounts are disputed
   - The evidence supporting each point
4. Submit materials to the **existing hearing record**, not as a new appeal. Adding materials does not itself create repayment terms, make a payment, withdraw your appeal, or change the financial obligation.

EDD’s official guidance says appeal submissions should explain the reasons for the appeal and include relevant documents and evidence. The hearing notice should also provide the submission instructions and location.

### Payment or waiver
Your account currently shows no collection notice and no online repayment enrollment. EDD states that online repayment generally becomes available only after a Benefit Overpayment Collection Notice containing a Claimant ID and Letter ID is issued. Do not make a payment or start an installment agreement merely because the deadline is approaching if you intend to continue disputing the determination; those are separate financial actions.

A waiver request is also a separate process. It may be worth considering if the overpayment was not your fault and repayment would cause extraordinary hardship, but it does not replace or automatically end the appeal.

I can help prepare materials for the scheduled hearing record. Please provide:

- The amount claimed and the reason EDD gave for the overpayment
- The weeks or payments you dispute
- The facts explaining why EDD is wrong
- The documents or evidence you want included

If you want me to prepare the submission, explicitly say: **“I authorize you to prepare and upload these materials to hearing record CUIAB-HR-778241-02.”**
